content.problemStatement:
Chef has called on you many times in the past to solve unusual tasks that
seem irrelevant to cooking, and today is no different.
Today Chef demands that you find integers whose product of digits is equal to a given integer,
but in bases besides 10.
Given a string S, you are to determine the smallest positive integer I such that there
exists a base B>1 for which the product of the digits of I
gives the integer represented by S.
For example, if S="11", then the smallest I is 8, because the base 3
representation of I is 22, and in base 3 2*2=11.


Formally, your task is this: find the smallest integer I such that there exists
an integer B>1 and integers Ai for which:



An*Bn+An-1*Bn-1+...+A1*B+A0 = I
0≤Ai**
****An≠0****
An*An-1*...*A1*A0 = 
Sm*Bm+Sm-1*Bm-1+...+S1*B1+S0,
where Sm is the leftmost character of S, and S0 is the rightmost character of S.
0≤Si**
********



Input
Input begins with an integer T, the number of test cases (at most 50).
Each test case consists of a single line containing a string S
comprised of 0-9 and A-Z characters.
Characters A-Z represent the decimal values 10-35, respectively.  S will have between 1 and 4 characters, and the first character will not be 0.



Output
For each test case, output a single integer on a line, giving the minimum
value of I (printed in decimal).
It is guaranteed that I will fit in a 64-bit integer.


Sample Input
4
9
11
7Q2
PIES

Sample Output
9
8
15227
7845414
****
constraints: S consists of 0-9 and A-Z characters
The first character of S is not 0
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of a single line containing a string S comprised of 0-9 and A-Z characters. Characters A-Z represent the decimal values 10-35, respectively.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the minimum value of I (printed in decimal) on a new line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the smallest I is 9 because the base 10 representation of I is 9 and the product of the digits is 9. For the second test case, the smallest I is 8 because the base 3 representation of I is 22 and the product of the digits is 2*2 = 4, but in base 3, 4 is represented as 11. For the third test case, the smallest I is 15227 because the base 36 representation of I is 7Q2 and the product of the digits is 7*17*2 = 238. For the fourth test case, the smallest I is 7845414 because the base 36 representation of I is PIES and the product of the digits is 15*8*4*13 = 6240, but in base 36, 6240 is represented as PIES.
The base 36 representation of I is A and the product of the digits is 10.
The base 10 representation of I is 1 and the product of the digits is 1.

solution.approach: The problem requires finding the smallest positive integer I such that there exists a base B>1 for which the product of the digits of I gives the integer represented by S. We can solve this by iterating over all possible bases (from 2 to 36) and for each base, we generate all possible numbers I until we find one that satisfies the condition.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * 36 * 10000) per test case, O(T * 36 * 10000) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the space used does not grow with the size of the input, only with the number of digits in the output.
